turns.content: I'm trying to build a DSL in my new Rust job inspired by sql!{} and html!{} macros, but I keep getting errors. I tried writing this function that takes &TokenStream and returns () with #[proc_macro] attached to it.

You need to understand that a proc-macro isn't just any Rust function; it must implement the ProcMacro trait from the proc-macro crate. Your function signature needs to match exactly what the compiler expects when you write #[proc_macro] on a module or item.
Oh okay, so I should just add that attribute? But my code still fails with 'cannot find symbol' errors. I thought any function with #[proc_macro] would automatically become a macro like sql!{}.

The issue is that #[proc_macro] must be placed on the actual module or item containing your logic, not just on a standalone function. You need to create a lib.rs file with impl ProcMacro for your proc-macro crate so the compiler knows how to invoke it.
Wait, do I need to implement something else? What exactly does the trait require?

You must implement one of the three traits: ProcMacro2018 for Rust 2018 edition, or more commonly now, the function-like proc-macro which implements a custom derive macro. For your DSL use case, you'll likely want to parse tokens using syn::parse::<YourStruct>(tokens) and then emit new tokens back with TokenStream.
That makes sense! But I'm still confused about how the token stream flows. Can someone explain exactly what happens when I write sql!{}?

When you write sql!{}, the macro expands into a TokenStream that represents your SQL query structure. The proc-macro receives this token stream, parses it using syn to build an internal representation of your DSL, and then outputs a new TokenStream with additional tokens or modified tokens.
So the macro gets the tokens from my input, processes them internally, and returns transformed tokens? Is that right?

Exactly. The proc-macro receives TokenStream as input, parses it to extract meaningful data, performs your DSL logic, then constructs a new TokenStream with the result and returns it via the trait method.
One more thing - I noticed my code has a syntax error. The compiler says 'unexpected token' when parsing the input tokens. What's wrong?

You're probably using the wrong parser or not handling whitespace correctly. Make sure you're using syn::parse from the correct version and that your DSL grammar matches what users will actually type into sql!{}. Also check if you're consuming all tokens properly.

I'm trying to understand how a function-like proc macro actually receives the tokens I pass inside my sql!{} syntax, but I keep getting confused about whether it gets a String or something else entirely.

You are confusing the input format with the runtime data type. The macro itself doesn't receive a String; instead, it accepts an arbitrary token stream which is represented as a Vec<TToken> in Rust's internal representation. When you write sql![], the preprocessor expands that into a concrete function call where your crate receives the tokens as a TokenStream parameter to the proc_macro::Item or similar trait implementation.
So if I want to parse those tokens, do I need to import something from syn specifically? I've been trying to write my own lexer but it feels like I'm reinventing the wheel unnecessarily.

You are absolutely right to avoid building a full parser from scratch; that is exactly why crates like syn exist. You should import syn::TokenStream and syn::parse to handle the token stream directly without writing your own lexer logic. The proc_macro trait implementation gets you the TokenStream, which you then feed into syn's parse functions.
Okay, so I need a function that implements the specific trait for proc macros? And can I just call parse on whatever string representation comes in?

Yes, your macro must implement the ProcMacro trait or satisfy the Item trait depending on whether you are expanding an item or processing attributes. Once you have the TokenStream from syn, you use functions like parse::<YourStruct>() to turn that token stream into a concrete Rust value.
What if my DSL needs custom error handling instead of just letting syn panic when tokens don't match?

You can wrap your parsing logic in a Result<T, syn::Error> and then convert those errors into user-friendly messages before returning them. The syn crate already provides detailed diagnostic information so you don't need to re-implement error reporting logic yourself.
I'm still worried about the difference between the token stream I get at compile time versus what runs at runtime when my code actually executes.

The distinction is important but they are connected: the TokenStream you receive during macro expansion represents your input syntax, while the resulting struct instance becomes part of your compiled binary. The proc_macro environment only exists at compile time to transform tokens into a valid Rust AST that can be executed later.
Can I chain multiple macros together in my DSL like sql!() then html!()? If so, how do I pass the intermediate structure forward?

You cannot directly nest macro invocations because each proc macro runs independently at different expansion points. Instead of nesting, you should define a builder pattern where your first macro returns an object that accepts further configuration calls before returning the final expanded AST.
That makes sense with builders but what if I want to support conditional logic like if this table exists then add it?

You can use procedural macros to inspect token streams and conditionally expand different code paths based on context. By analyzing tokens early in the expansion phase, you can decide whether to include certain clauses or skip them entirely before generating the final output.
This is getting complicated but I think the key insight is that proc macros operate purely syntactically without needing runtime state?

Exactly, proc macros work exclusively on syntax trees and never interact with runtime variables. Your entire logic must be implemented using token analysis, pattern matching against AST nodes, and string interpolation within the macro definition itself.
One last thing: how do I debug a complex expansion when my output looks wrong?

You should use cargo expand to see exactly what your proc macro generates before compilation. This tool reveals the full expanded code allowing you to verify that your token parsing and conditional logic produced the expected AST structure.

I'm trying to build a macro for my side project like sql!{} or html!{}, but I keep getting an error about the token stream. The compiler says it expects a function-like proc macro but I only wrote #[proc_macro] and tried to return raw strings directly. What went wrong?

The issue is you're treating the proc macro as if it were just a regular function that returns a string, which won't work for your use case. A function-like proc macro in Rust must accept a token stream via the TokenStream type and then return a new TokenStream containing the generated code. You cannot simply return a String or raw string literal because the macro system needs to manipulate syntax trees at compile time rather than generate output text directly. The correct signature requires returning syn::TokenStream, not std::string::String.
Okay so I need to parse tokens and build new ones? That sounds really complicated for a weekend project when I'm used to scripting where macros just expand inline without this token parsing stuff.

It does feel more complex than scripting languages, but once you understand the token stream concept it becomes manageable. Think of the TokenStream as a list of tokens that represents source code syntax. Your macro receives these tokens via syn::TokenStream and can analyze them to build a new TokenStream with modified or added elements. This allows your macro to look like sql!{table users; select *} where the input is parsed into internal representation, transformed by your logic, then converted back into token stream output that looks exactly like valid SQL to the compiler.

I thought I could just write a normal function and use #[proc_macro] on it to handle token streams like sql!{}, but my Rust compiler is screaming at me because the signature doesn't match what I expected. I need help understanding why a regular impl trait won't work here.

That's a common stumbling block for beginners. A #[proc_macro] attribute must be attached to a function, not an implementation of a trait or a regular method on a struct. The compiler requires the proc macro to be defined as a standalone function that accepts exactly one argument of type TokenStream and returns a TokenStream. You cannot use impl Trait or any other trait object in place of this specific signature because proc-macros run in isolation from your normal crate context. They need to implement the ProcMacro trait implicitly, which mandates this exact input/output contract.

Okay so I can't put #[proc_macro] on an impl block? That feels like a hard restriction when I want to build a DSL similar to sql!{} or html!{}. How do I actually structure the code if I can't use normal Rust syntax for this function?

You absolutely can and should use normal Rust syntax. The restriction is purely on where the attribute lives, not what you write inside. You define a regular function outside of any impl block with #[proc_macro] as its first line, then declare the parameter as TokenStream and the return type as TokenStream. Inside that function, you parse the token stream using syn::parse or manual parsing logic to build your DSL's internal representation, then emit the desired tokens back out. The key is that the function itself must be a top-level definition in your crate.
I'm getting confused about how TokenStream actually works since it seems like a dynamic thing rather than a concrete type I can manipulate directly without syn or similar crates.

TokenStream isn't a concrete Rust type you define; it's a wrapper provided by the proc-macro crate itself that represents an arbitrary sequence of tokens. To work with it, you need to import TokenStream from the proc-macro crate and use syn::parse for parsing logic. The crate provides traits like Parse for types that can be converted into TokenStreams. You cannot define your own type to replace TokenStream because proc-macros are designed to accept whatever token stream is passed by the compiler, so you must rely on existing crates like syn or custom parser implementations.

I'm frustrated because I've been trying to wrap this in a trait implementation and it keeps failing my build. It feels like there's a fundamental misunderstanding about how Rust handles these low-level expansions that makes me feel stuck. Can we really just use normal functions? I keep thinking I need something more advanced.

It is frustrating when you hit this wall, but the solution is simpler than it seems. You do not need traits or complex abstractions; a standard function with #[proc_macro] at the top level is exactly what is required. The confusion comes from expecting proc-macros to behave like normal methods on structs. Once you define a plain function with the correct signature and attach #[proc_macro], it will run in the proc macro environment and receive TokenStream arguments naturally without needing any special trait implementations beyond the implicit ProcMacro trait that the compiler provides automatically.

I still don't understand why the compiler is so strict about this design choice if I'm just building a DSL like sql!{}. What happens if I try to pass an impl Trait as the return type? Why does it have to be TokenStream specifically and not something else?

The compiler is strict because proc-macros are extensions of code that exist outside your normal compilation unit; they receive raw tokens from the parser before any types are resolved. Returning a concrete Rust type like Vec or Option would require the macro to know which specific type you want, but TokenStream allows it to be generic over whatever output the compiler expects. If you tried to use impl Trait as return, the function wouldn't even compile because the proc-macro environment doesn't have access to your crate's trait definitions yet. You must always return exactly TokenStream so the macro can inject tokens back into the source code without needing type parameters.

I feel like I'm missing something obvious about how this fits into my larger architecture since I've been trying to use complex generics and closures instead of just a simple function call. Is there any way to make this more flexible if I really want to support multiple output formats?

The flexibility comes from using syn::parse to convert the input TokenStream into an intermediate representation, then emitting different outputs via separate code paths within your function. You can handle multiple cases inside the single #[proc_macro] function by checking the token stream structure and branching logic accordingly. The macro itself remains a single function, but you implement the DSL's internal state machine or parser there to produce whatever output format is needed, all while adhering to the fixed TokenStream input/output contract that defines its boundaries.

This makes me realize I was overcomplicating things with closures and traits when I should have just focused on parsing logic inside a normal function. But now I'm worried about how this scales if my DSL grows beyond simple cases. Can you help me understand the lifecycle of TokenStream from input to output in more detail?

TokenStream enters your #[proc_macro] function as the sole argument, representing the parsed source code tokens. Inside, you use syn::parse or manual iteration to convert it into an internal data structure like a Vec or enum variant that represents your DSL's grammar rules. Once processed, you construct a new TokenStream from this internal representation using syn::TokenTree or similar mechanisms and return it. The lifecycle is entirely contained within the function scope: input tokens get parsed into memory, then transformed back into output tokens before being handed to the compiler for code generation.

Thank you for clarifying the token flow, but I'm still struggling with how to handle errors if my parsing logic fails mid-function. Should I panic or return a specific TokenStream that signals an error? What is the best practice here?

You should never panic in a proc macro because it causes immediate compilation failure and makes debugging impossible for users. Instead, you construct a TokenStream containing error tokens from crates like syn::Error or custom parser errors. This allows the macro to continue running and return an invalid token sequence that the compiler can report back with detailed diagnostics. The best practice is to use syn's Parse trait implementations which provide ergonomic error handling via Result<T, Error>, then convert those results into TokenStreams containing meaningful error messages before returning them.

I understand now, but I'm still confused about how this fits with my existing codebase that uses regular functions. Does the #[proc_macro] attribute somehow intercept calls to my normal functions? I feel like there's a disconnect between my Rust knowledge and what I need here.

The #[proc_macro] attribute does not intercept your normal function calls in any way; it is purely a compiler directive that tells the Rust compiler to treat that specific function as a macro expansion point. Your regular functions remain unchanged and callable normally after compilation. The proc macro runs only when the compiler processes code containing macros like sql!{} or html!{}. There is no interference with your standard library usage or other crate dependencies; it simply adds an extra layer of preprocessing before the final binary is generated.

I finally get it, but I'm still worried about performance since I was trying to use closures and traits for optimization. Is there any overhead in using TokenStream compared to my previous approach? Can we really claim this is efficient enough for game development?

Yes, this approach is highly efficient because proc-macros run at compile time, not runtime. There is no performance penalty when calling macros in your game code; the overhead only exists during compilation when you build the crate. TokenStream operations involve basic string manipulation and AST traversal which are fast enough for most DSLs like sql!{}. If you need further optimization, focus on efficient parsing logic inside your macro rather than trying to avoid TokenStream entirely, since that is the intended mechanism for token manipulation in Rust.

Perfect. I think I can now refactor my code using a simple function with #[proc_macro] instead of those complex closures and traits I was stuck on earlier. Thank you for walking me through this step by step.

You are very welcome! Refactoring to use a standard function with #[proc_macro] is exactly the right move, as it removes unnecessary complexity while adhering to Rust's design goals of clarity and performance. If you encounter specific error messages during implementation, feel free to ask about them since debugging proc-macro signatures often requires looking at exact compiler output details.

I'm trying to build a custom SQL builder like sql!{} but I keep hitting errors. It feels kind of like writing a custom regex engine, except instead of matching patterns in text, I want to match and transform tokens in my code.

That's a great mental model for token streams! You're essentially building a parser generator. The key difference is that unlike regex which operates on strings, you operate on the raw syntax tree or token stream provided by the compiler. This allows you to inspect every part of your code, not just text patterns.
I tried defining a function with #[proc_macro] and passing the tokens in, but it's throwing an error about missing attributes. I thought maybe I needed to import something from the proc-macro crate?

Ah, you are likely confusing the attribute syntax with the actual trait implementation. The #[proc_macro] attribute is just a marker; it doesn't automatically give you access to tokens. You need to actually implement the FunctionLikeProcMacro trait in your crate and then define your macro using #[macro_export]. Let's walk through that.
Wait, I thought proc-macro was just a function? So when I call sql!{}, it somehow knows what to do?

Exactly, but not the way you might think. The macro expansion happens at compile time before your code even runs. When the compiler sees sql!{}, it calls your FunctionLikeProcMacro trait implementation which receives a TokenStream input. This allows you to build a DSL where users write natural-looking SQL and your macro translates it into valid Rust code that executes.
So the tokens come from the compiler, not me? I can't just pass strings around like in regular macros?

Correct. Regular macros accept string literals or expressions, but a proc-macro receives the actual token stream as a TokenStream type. This means you have full control over parsing and transforming the code structure itself.
I'm getting confused about the difference between #[proc_macro] and #[macro_export]. Which one do I actually need for my sql!{} macro?
You need both. The #[proc_macro] attribute is where you define your trait implementation, while #[macro_export] tells the compiler to export that function as a usable macro in your crate's namespace.
So I write the trait impl with #[proc_macro], then add #[macro_export] above my function name? And then the sql!{} macro is available to users?

Precisely. You implement the FunctionLikeProcMacro trait using #[proc_macro], then use #[macro_export] on your function declaration so other crates can call it via sql!{}. The compiler will automatically wire everything together for you.
One last thing, I want to parse the token stream and check if there's a SELECT statement. Is TokenStream just a string?

No, TokenStream is more like a list of tokens in order - each with its kind (identifier, punctuation) and value. You can iterate through it using the IntoIterator trait to check for specific elements, making your DSL much more powerful than simple string matching.
